name: Khan Academy
overview: Khan Academy is a non-profit online learning platform serving 30M+ monthly visitors and 180M+ registered users worldwide. Its core product loop is built on Bloom-style Mastery Learning (Not Started -> Familiar -> Proficient -> Mastered) with explicit Mastery Points per skill. A separate Energy Points system measures effort only, and Khan Academy is unusually transparent that Energy Points are 'not a measure of mastery or ability,' a deliberate design choice that decouples extrinsic engagement metrics from learning evidence and avoids Black Hat dark-pattern reinforcement loops.
originator_or_company: Khan Academy (501(c)(3) non-profit, founded 2008 by Salman 'Sal' Khan; design system 'Wonder Blocks' built by the Khan Academy product/design team).
key_resources: [What are energy points, badges, and avatars? - Khan Academy Help Center](https://support.khanacademy.org/hc/en-us/articles/202487710-What-are-energy-points-badges-and-avatars)
[How do Khan Academy's Mastery levels work? - Help Center](https://support.khanacademy.org/hc/en-us/articles/5548760867853--How-do-Khan-Academy-s-Mastery-levels-work)
[Sal Khan TED Talk: Let's teach for mastery -- not test scores](https://www.ted.com/talks/sal_khan_let_s_teach_for_mastery_not_test_scores)
[Wonder Blocks: on the creation of Khan Academy's design system](https://www.designsystems.com/about-wonder-blocks-khan-academys-design-system-and-the-story-behind-it/)
[A Critique of 'Gamification' in Khan Academy - DiSalvo & Morrison](https://digitalcommons.unomaha.edu/compsicfacproc/54/)
[Khan Academy: eLearning Gamification through an Octalysis lens](https://octalysisgroup.com/2015/09/khan-academy-elearning-gamification-through-an-octalysis-lens/)
[How We Rebuilt Khan Academy's Color System from the Ground Up](https://blog.khanacademy.org/how-we-rebuilt-khan-academys-color-system-from-the-ground-up/)
core_mechanics: Two parallel reward currencies, deliberately separated: (1) MASTERY POINTS - per-skill progress with four discrete levels: Not Started/Attempted (first attempt below 70%), Familiar (70-85% on first try, +50 Mastery Points), Proficient (100% on a single exercise/quiz starting from Familiar, +30 -> total 80), Mastered (100% on Unit Test or Course Challenge from Proficient, +20 -> total 100). Each skill is worth 100 Mastery Points; course mastery is the aggregate. Mastery Challenges resurface previously learned skills using spaced retrieval to confirm retention. (2) ENERGY POINTS - effort currency awarded for watching videos (~750 first watch + 100 completion bonus), correctly answering practice problems (~50 per Mastery Challenge problem), completing programming challenges, earning badges, and participating in discussions. Energy Points unlock cosmetic rewards: avatars, profile backgrounds, and badges (Meteorite, Moon, Earth, Sun, Black Hole, and Challenge Patches). Critically, Energy Points are explicitly framed as effort, not skill, and were intentionally REMOVED from teacher-facing dashboards because they were 'not a very good measure of mastery progress or activity.' UI surfaces: Course/Unit page mastery bars (segmented progress meters per skill), Mastery Challenge prompt cards, the persistent Energy Points counter in the top bar, and the avatar/badge wall on the user profile. The Wonder Blocks design system standardizes these surfaces with a semantic color token system (Intensity Scale), accessible button hierarchy with one primary action color, and a tightened type ramp (5 typefaces, 14 type styles).
designer_or_researcher_voice: Sal Khan (founder): 'Mastery learning simply means allowing a student to continue to work on a concept until they can master that concept or skill.' - Why Mastery Learning essay, Khan Academy Help Center.

Sal Khan (TED 2016, 'Let's teach for mastery -- not test scores'): the framing is that traditional schooling fixes time and varies outcome, while mastery learning fixes outcome (every student masters every topic) and varies time.

Khan Academy Help Center (official product copy): 'Energy points measure effort on Khan Academy. Learners earn more energy points by pushing the edge of their knowledge. Most importantly, they are not a measure of mastery or ability.' This is a rare instance of a product publicly disclaiming its own gamified currency.
Khan Academy product team, on removing Energy Points from teacher views: '[Energy points] were not a very good measure of mastery progress or activity,' which led to their removal from teacher views.

Wonder Blocks design team (designsystems.com case study): describes the redesign goal as 'clean, structured modern design,' avoiding 'clutter and sloppy craft,' and fusing 'minimalism and efficiency with the approachability of friendly classroom decor' - i.e., engagement should come from clarity, not from FOMO or streak pressure.
metrics_or_evidence: 30M+ monthly visitors, 120M+ yearly active learners, 180M+ registered users globally (2025). 14 minutes/day average app usage in 2024. Khanmigo grew from 68K pilot users (2023-24) to 700K+ users (2024-25). EFFICACY: a 2022 peer-reviewed PNAS study showed students using Khan Academy 30-60 min/week beat pre-pandemic growth norms by up to 40% in some grades. RCT in India across ~5,500 students / 74 schools: high-usage cohorts scored +0.44 to +0.47 SD higher than control on end-of-year math. A Newark, NJ three-year panel: +18 hrs / +60 skills mastered nearly doubled NJSLA gains. A year-long mastery learning intervention improved end-of-year math scores by +0.12 to +0.22 SD. (Sources: blog.khanacademy.org/research, Khan Academy Efficacy Results Nov 2024.) [uncertain] No public RCT isolates the Energy Points system specifically from the Mastery Learning system; gains are attributed to mastery learning + dosage, not to badges/points.
criticisms: DiSalvo & Morrison's academic critique 'A Critique of "Gamification" in Khan Academy' argues the badge/points layer is bolted on rather than integrated with learning theory. The Octalysis Group analysis concludes Khan's gamification is 'unbalanced, geared towards left brain and extrinsic motivation with not much intrinsic motivational design,' lacks unpredictability/epic-meaning hooks, and risks failing to sustain long-term engagement. Broader behavioral economics: Deci, Koestner & Ryan (1999) meta-analysis (128 studies) shows tangible extrinsic rewards undermine intrinsic motivation (d = -0.34), and Lepper et al. (1973) marker study found a 50% drop in free-play drawing after expected reward - the classic Overjustification Effect risk for any points system overlaid on learning. Khan Academy's saving grace is that Energy Points are explicitly informational (effort acknowledgement), which Deci's framework predicts is less corrosive than controlling rewards. User-side complaint: the persistent Energy Points banner has been called distracting and 'early-2000s gamification style,' covering parts of equations during videos.
failure_cases: Energy Points were REMOVED from teacher dashboards because they were not a meaningful proxy for learning progress - an explicit, public retreat from gamification-as-measurement. Several legacy badges tied to the deprecated 'Mission' system were retired when Khan Academy moved permanently to Course Mastery. Community forum threads ('Skills, Badges and Energy Points (gone wrong)', 'Can we bring back badges?') document user-side regression after badge cleanup. The Energy Points Store concept was discussed in community posts but never shipped at scale. The 2023 Course/Unit page redesign also reduced visual emphasis on points in favor of mastery bars.
antipatterns: 1) Conflating effort currency with proficiency - the original sin Khan Academy course-corrected by disclaiming Energy Points as not-mastery. 2) Persistent on-screen counter overlaying learning content (the Energy Points banner over video equations) - a Black Hat 'always-visible reward tracker' pattern that hijacks attention. 3) Leaderboards (Khan once had global Energy Point leaderboards) which incentivize grinding low-value tasks ('Energy Point Grinding' wiki page documents exploits). 4) Badge inflation without retrospective meaning - retiring badges later forces sunset communication and user disappointment. 5) Tying cosmetic rewards (avatars/backgrounds) to raw point thresholds rather than to mastery milestones - aesthetically motivating but semantically empty. 6) Means-Ends Reversal risk: students optimizing for points/badges rather than understanding (overjustification effect).
applicability_to_shikaq: HIGHLY ADAPTABLE with strong philosophical alignment. The two-currency split (effort vs. mastery) maps cleanly onto shikaq's domain: (a) MASTERY layer - per-section/per-topic skill mastery using IT-Pass / FE / SPI / Boki taxonomies, with discrete levels (Familiar -> Proficient -> Mastered) tied to spaced-repetition retests. This is the Apple Fitness 'rings + activity history' analogue already in our design map. (b) EFFORT layer - a soft, non-leaderboard 'today's effort' indicator (minutes studied, problems attempted) presented in the Today screen and the Summary view, deliberately decoupled from monetary or status rewards. Match to HEXAD user types: Achievers (mastery levels), Philanthropists (helping community via shared notes), Free Spirits (self-paced re-attempt), Players (subtle effort indicator) - explicitly avoid catering to Disruptors. For 20s women / cafe / iPad context, lean Apple Fitness aesthetic: a soft mastery ring per certification, no aggressive streak guilt, no global leaderboards. Use SF Symbols 'checkmark.seal.fill' for Mastered, 'sparkles' for Proficient, segmented circular progress for partial mastery. Avoid Khan's persistent Energy Points banner pattern entirely - it conflicts with Apple HIG Deference. Surface mastery only on Summary and Practice review screens; Today screen shows the next learnable skill, not a points counter.
phase_assignment: Phase 1 (3 weeks) for the MASTERY LEVEL data model and basic per-skill progress UI (Not Started / Familiar / Proficient / Mastered) - this is foundational and cannot be retrofitted easily. Phase 2 (4-6 weeks) for spaced Mastery Challenges and the Summary ring visualization. Phase 3 (6-8 weeks) for any soft effort indicator, deliberately deferred to validate that mastery alone drives retention before adding any extrinsic layer.
implementation_size: M (medium). Mastery state machine, per-skill points table, spaced-repetition scheduler, and Summary ring visualization. Heavier than a simple badge system but lighter than a full SRS engine because the level transitions are deterministic and rule-based.
integration_points: DB: extend `progress` table with `mastery_level` enum ('not_started'|'attempted'|'familiar'|'proficient'|'mastered') and `mastery_points` int per (user_id, skill_id) where skill_id is a finer grain than lesson_id (likely a new `skills` table or a `lessons.skill_tag`); add `mastery_challenges` table for spaced retests; `quiz_results` already supports the level-up logic. Components: Summary screen Apple Fitness-style mastery rings (extend `ActivityRings.tsx` or new `MasteryRings.tsx`), Practice screen end-of-session level-up animation, Today screen 'next skill to master' card via `TodayContent.tsx`, Sidebar certification badge gets a small mastery percentage ring overlay. Routes: new `/summary/mastery` deep-link target. RLS: per-user mastery rows under standard `auth.uid() = user_id` policy. NO global leaderboard table - explicit non-feature.
risks: 1) ETHICAL/PERSONA: Energy-point style mechanics risk feeling juvenile to a 20s career-track audience - mitigated by importing only the mastery layer, not the badge/avatar layer. 2) IMPLEMENTATION: defining the right grain of 'skill' for IT-Pass / FE / SPI / Boki is non-trivial; too coarse and mastery feels unearned, too fine and the UI fragments. 3) SCOPE CREEP: it is tempting to also build badges, avatars, and stores - resist. 4) APPLE HIG CONFLICT: Khan's persistent counter banner directly violates Deference; we must NOT replicate that surface. 5) OVERJUSTIFICATION: even mastery points can backfire if framed as reward rather than feedback - copy must be informational ('You've mastered Subnetting'), not transactional ('+100 pts!'). 6) [uncertain] Lack of isolated efficacy data for the points layer means we cannot confidently estimate retention lift from importing it.
apple_hig_compatibility: needs_adaptation. The Mastery Learning data model and level taxonomy translate cleanly to Apple HIG (Apple Fitness rings, Books reading goals, Reminders completion checks all use similar mastery-as-completion patterns). The Energy Points UI surfaces (persistent top-bar counter, badge wall, avatar shop, leaderboard) directly conflict with Clarity, Deference, and Less-but-better. Recommended adaptation: keep Mastery, drop persistent counters, replace badges with subtle SF Symbol completion seals, replace avatars with no analogue, render mastery as Activity Rings and segmented progress bars only. Use Liquid Glass on Practice toolbar; never on the mastery progress itself (must remain crisp and content-foreground).
ethical_score: positive (with caveats). Khan Academy's explicit decoupling of Energy Points from mastery, and the public removal of points from teacher views, is a rare case of a learning product course-correcting AWAY from Black Hat reinforcement. The Mastery Learning core is informational feedback aligned with Self-Determination Theory's competence-acknowledgement (Deci/Ryan), low burnout risk, low time-regret risk. CAVEAT: the legacy Energy Points banner and badge/leaderboard surfaces, where preserved, still risk overjustification and grinding behavior - shikaq must import the mastery layer cleanly without the points-banner UI to maintain a positive ethical score.
evidence_quality: rigorous_RCT (for the mastery learning core: PNAS 2022 peer-reviewed study, India RCT n~5500/74 schools, Newark NJ three-year panel). For the gamification/points layer specifically: theoretical + company_case_study only - no published RCT isolates Energy Points/badges from the broader platform.
localization_fit_japan: high. Mastery learning maps onto a deeply familiar Japanese pedagogical frame ('shikaku' / 級・段 progression, 検定 ladder, 漢検/英検 graded mastery), making the Familiar -> Proficient -> Mastered ladder culturally resonant. The 20s career-track woman in a cafe wants to track concrete mastery, not collect cosmetic badges - so importing Mastery and dropping the gamification layer is well-matched to the persona. AVOID: global leaderboards (low fit, can feel showy/embarrassing in JP context) and avatar shops (feels juvenile for the persona). Use understated Japanese-typography-friendly progress copy ('習得済み' / '練習中' / '未学習') over English point totals.
